name: Volume — How Much Space Inside
whatIsIt: VOLUME is how much space a solid takes up — how much it can hold. We measure it by counting equal UNIT CUBES that fill it. For a box (cuboid), one layer has length × breadth cubes, and there are 'height' layers, so volume = length × breadth × height cubic units.
whyNeeded: Volume tells us how much fits: water in a tank, rice in a box, bricks in a lorry. Area covers a flat floor, but volume fills a space — it is the step from flat measuring to 3D measuring, and it powers capacity (litres) too.
realLifeUses: How many small boxes fit inside a big carton
How much water a tank or bottle can hold
Stacking sugar cubes or bricks in a crate
Filling a matchbox with tiny cubes to measure its space

vocabulary.meaning: The amount of space a solid takes up, measured in cubic units.
A cube of side 1 unit — the building block for measuring volume.
The unit of volume, e.g., cubic centimetre (cm³).
One flat sheet of cubes; stack the layers to fill the box.

story.title: Filling the Box with Sugar Cubes
story.text: Meena fills a small box with sugar cubes. One layer on the bottom holds 3 rows of 2 — that is 6 cubes. 'And how many layers fit on top?' asks Ajji. 'Three layers!' 'So 6 in a layer, three layers high: 6, 12, 18 cubes fill it. Volume is length × breadth × height — count the cubes that fill the whole space.'
story.extractedProblem: A box holds 3 rows of 2 cubes in one layer, and 3 layers high. How many unit cubes fill it?
story.answerInStory: 18 cubes — one layer is 3 × 2 = 6, times 3 layers = 18 cubic units.

visual.caption: One layer = 3 × 2 = 6 cubes. Stack 3 layers → volume = 6 × 3 = 18 cubic units.

teachingGallery.title: Volume — how many cubes fit inside
teachingGallery.note: Fill the box with unit cubes: length × breadth × height.

standardMethod.summary: Count unit cubes: one layer is length × breadth cubes; multiply by the number of layers (height). Volume = L × B × H.
steps: Find how many cubes fit in ONE bottom layer: length × breadth.
Count how many layers stack up: the height.
Multiply: layer cubes × number of layers.
Volume = length × breadth × height, in cubic units.

alternateMethods.name: Layer-by-layer
steps: Count the cubes in the bottom layer.
Add another identical layer, and another, up to the height.
Skip-count the layer total by the number of layers.

alternateMethods.name: Area × height
steps: Find the area of the base (length × breadth).
Multiply the base area by the height.

workedExamples.problem: A box is 4 units long, 2 units wide, and 3 units high. How many unit cubes fill it?
A cube is 2 units on every side. What is its volume?

trickPractice.intro: Volume = area of the base times how many layers tall — count the cubes.

questions.q: A box is 2 long, 3 wide, 2 high. How many unit cubes fit?
A box is 3 long, 3 wide, 1 high. How many unit cubes fit?
A box is 2 long, 4 wide, 3 high. How many unit cubes fit?
A box is 2 long, 2 wide, 2 high. How many unit cubes fit?
A box is 5 long, 1 wide, 1 high. How many unit cubes fit?
A box is 3 long, 2 wide, 4 high. How many unit cubes fit?

commonMistakes.mistake: Multiplying only length × breadth and forgetting the height.
commonMistakes.fix: Area is flat (L × B). VOLUME fills space, so also multiply by the HEIGHT: L × B × H. Don't stop at one layer.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the three sides (4 + 2 + 3) instead of multiplying.
commonMistakes.fix: Volume counts cubes that fill the box, so MULTIPLY the three measurements: 4 × 2 × 3 = 24, not 4 + 2 + 3.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving the answer in square units instead of cubic units.
commonMistakes.fix: Volume fills 3D space, so the unit is CUBIC units (cm³), not square units. Area is square; volume is cubic.

easy.q: One layer has 6 cubes and there are 2 layers. How many cubes in total?
A box is 3 long, 2 wide, 1 high. Volume in cubic units?

medium.q: A box is 4 long, 2 wide, 3 high. Volume?
A cube has sides of 3 units. Volume?

challenge.q: A box is 5 long, 3 wide, 2 high. Volume in cubic units?
The base area of a box is 12 and its height is 4. What is the volume?

questions.q: Layer of 4 cubes, 2 layers. Total cubes?

teachBackPrompt: Stack sugar cubes or small boxes into a bigger box. Teach someone at home how to find the VOLUME by counting one layer, then multiplying by the number of layers — and why volume uses CUBIC units, not square.

revisionCard.summary: Volume = space inside, counted in UNIT CUBES. One layer = length × breadth; times the height (layers). Volume = L × B × H cubic units. Multiply, don't add; cubic, not square.

gameMission.title: Filling the Matchbox
gameMission.brief: Stack unit cubes layer by layer to measure how much each box can hold.

realLifeProject: Find a small box and some sugar cubes or dice. Fill the bottom layer, count it, then count the layers, and work out the box's volume.
